title: K closest elements
description: Find k closest elements using heap

examples.explanation: Step 1: Compute absolute differences: |1-3|=2, |2-3|=1, |3-3|=0, |4-3|=1, |5-3|=2. Step 2: Create pairs: [(2,1), (1,2), (0,3), (1,4), (2,5)]. Step 3: Sort pairs by difference: [(0,3), (1,2), (1,4), (2,1), (2,5)]. Step 4: Extract first k=4 elements: 3, 2, 4, 1. Step 5: Sort result: [1, 2, 3, 4].

Step 1: Compute absolute differences: |1-(-1)|=2, |2-(-1)|=3, |3-(-1)|=4, |4-(-1)|=5, |5-(-1)|=6. Step 2: Create pairs: [(2,1), (3,2), (4,3), (5,4), (6,5)]. Step 3: Sort pairs by difference: [(2,1), (3,2), (4,3), (5,4), (6,5)]. Step 4: Extract first k=2 elements: 1, 2. Step 5: Sort result: [1, 2].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Compute absolute differences from target for all elements, sort them, and pick the k smallest. Use this for simplicity when k is small or n is moderate.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find K Closest Elements Using Brute Force O(n log n)
ladders.desc: Complete solution: compute absolute differences from target for all elements, sort them, and pick k smallest.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding k closest elements is straightforward: for each element in the array, compute its absolute difference from the target value x. This difference represents how 'far' the element is from x. We then sort all these differences along with their corresponding elements, and finally select the k smallest differences to get the k closest elements. The intuition is simple: by sorting all differences, we can easily identify which elements have the smallest distances from x. This approach works because the absolute difference function preserves order - if element A is closer to x than element B, then |A-x| < |B-x|. The algorithm proceeds in three clear steps: 1) Create pairs of (difference, element) for all elements, 2) Sort these pairs by difference, 3) Extract the first k elements from the sorted list. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to sorting, and space complexity is O(n) for storing the pairs. This approach is particularly useful when k is small relative to n or when n is moderate, as it's simple to implement and understand. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle ties properly (when two elements have equal distance from x), not considering edge cases like empty arrays or k=0, and incorrectly implementing the absolute difference calculation. The connection to the parent problem is direct - this is the complete solution using the brute force approach.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute absolute differences: |1-3|=2, |2-3|=1, |3-3|=0, |4-3|=1, |5-3|=2. Step 2: Create pairs: [(2,1), (1,2), (0,3), (1,4), (2,5)]. Step 3: Sort pairs by difference: [(0,3), (1,2), (1,4), (2,1), (2,5)]. Step 4: Extract first k=4 elements: 3, 2, 4, 1. Step 5: Sort result: [1, 2, 3, 4].

Step 1: Compute absolute differences: |1-(-1)|=2, |2-(-1)|=3, |3-(-1)|=4, |4-(-1)|=5, |5-(-1)|=6. Step 2: Create pairs: [(2,1), (3,2), (4,3), (5,4), (6,5)]. Step 3: Sort pairs by difference: [(2,1), (3,2), (4,3), (5,4), (6,5)]. Step 4: Extract first k=2 elements: 1, 2. Step 5: Sort result: [1, 2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sort Pairs by Absolute Difference
ladders.desc: The most important helper: sort (difference, element) pairs by difference value.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. After computing absolute differences for all elements, we need to sort these differences along with their corresponding elements to identify the k smallest distances. The intuition is that sorting brings order to chaos - by arranging all differences in ascending order, the smallest differences (representing closest elements) naturally come first. We create pairs of (difference, element) because we need to preserve the association between each difference and its original element. The algorithm: 1) Create an empty list 'pairs', 2) For each element in array, compute absolute difference from x and append (difference, element) to pairs, 3) Sort pairs by the first element (difference) using any stable sorting algorithm. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting, space is O(n) for storing pairs. This feeds directly into the full problem - once sorted, we can simply take the first k elements. Common mistakes include sorting only differences without preserving element association, using unstable sort that might scramble equal differences, and not handling empty arrays. The real pattern involves list comprehension and sorted() with key parameter.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute differences: |1-2|=1, |2-2|=0, |3-2|=1. Step 2: Create pairs: [(1,1), (0,2), (1,3)]. Step 3: Sort by first element: compare 1,0,1 → 0 comes first, then the two 1s maintain original order (stable sort): [(0,2), (1,1), (1,3)].
Step 1: Compute differences: |5-4|=1, |3-4|=1, |1-4|=3. Step 2: Create pairs: [(1,5), (1,3), (3,1)]. Step 3: Sort by first element: both 1s come before 3, maintaining order: [(1,5), (1,3), (3,1)].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Absolute Differences from Target
ladders.desc: Central technique: calculate |element - x| for each array element.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental operation that enables distance comparison. The absolute difference |a-x| measures how far element a is from target x, regardless of whether a is greater or less than x. The intuition is that distance is always non-negative and symmetric - an element 2 units below x is just as 'close' as an element 2 units above x. Mathematically, |a-x| = a-x if a ≥ x, or x-a if a < x. This transforms the problem from finding elements with values near x to finding elements with small absolute differences. The algorithm is simple: iterate through the array, for each element compute abs(element - x). Time complexity is O(n), space is O(1) if we just compute (not store). This feeds into the sub-routine by providing the raw distance data that needs sorting. Common mistakes include using squared differences (which changes scale), forgetting absolute value (which would treat elements below x as negative distances), and integer overflow with large numbers. The real pattern is a simple loop with abs() function.

examples.trace: Step 1: For element 1: |1-5| = 4. Step 2: For element 5: |5-5| = 0. Step 3: For element 9: |9-5| = 4. Result: [4, 0, 4].
Step 1: For element 3: |3-4| = 1. Step 2: For element 7: |7-4| = 3. Step 3: For element 2: |2-4| = 2. Result: [1, 3, 2].

ladders.title: Building Block — Sort List of Numbers
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept: arrange numbers in ascending order.
ladders.explanation: Sorting is a fundamental algorithmic building block. The intuition is that ordered data is easier to process than unordered data - we can quickly find minimums, maximums, medians, and identify patterns. For this problem, we need to sort differences to find the k smallest ones. Any comparison-based sort (like quicksort, mergesort, or heapsort) will work, typically achieving O(n log n) time. The algorithm: take an unsorted list, repeatedly compare and swap elements until the entire list is in non-decreasing order. Most programming languages provide built-in sort functions that implement efficient algorithms. Time complexity is O(n log n) for comparison sorts, space varies (O(1) for heapsort, O(n) for mergesort). This feeds into the core logic by enabling efficient identification of smallest differences. Common mistakes include assuming sort is O(n), not considering stability requirements, and incorrect comparison functions. The real pattern is simply calling sorted() or .sort().

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 3 and 1: 3>1, swap → [1, 3, 4, 2]. Step 2: Compare 3 and 4: 3<4, no swap. Step 3: Compare 4 and 2: 4>2, swap → [1, 3, 2, 4]. Step 4: Compare 3 and 2: 3>2, swap → [1, 2, 3, 4]. Step 5: Verify sorted: 1≤2≤3≤4.

Step 1: Compare 5 and 5: equal, no swap. Step 2: Compare 5 and 3: 5>3, swap → [5, 3, 5, 7]. Step 3: Compare 5 and 7: 5<7, no swap. Step 4: Compare 5 and 3: 5>3, swap → [3, 5, 5, 7]. Step 5: Verify sorted: 3≤5≤5≤7.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Calculate Absolute Value
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation: compute |a| = a if a≥0, -a if a<0.
ladders.explanation: The absolute value operation is mathematical foundation for distance calculation. Intuition: absolute value removes the sign, giving only magnitude. For any real number a, |a| is its distance from 0 on the number line. In programming, abs() function typically implements: return a if a >= 0 else -a. This is crucial for our problem because we want distance from x, not signed difference. Time complexity is O(1), space O(1). This feeds into the building block as the core computation for each element. Common mistakes include implementing incorrectly for negative numbers, integer overflow when negating minimum integer, and confusion with floating point precision. The real pattern is using built-in abs() or simple conditional.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if 5 ≥ 0: yes. Step 2: Return 5.
Step 1: Check if -3 ≥ 0: no. Step 2: Return -(-3) = 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Number Line Distance
ladders.desc: Simplest prerequisite: distance between two numbers on number line.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic concept: on a number line, the distance between numbers a and b is how far apart they are, always non-negative. Intuition: imagine a ruler - the distance from 3 to 5 is 2 units, same as from 5 to 3. Mathematically, distance = |a-b|. For example, distance between 7 and 10 is |7-10| = 3. This foundational concept underpins the entire problem - we're finding elements with smallest distance to x. No algorithm needed, just conceptual understanding. This feeds into basic operation as the motivation for absolute value. Common misunderstandings include thinking distance can be negative, or confusing distance with signed difference. Examples should visualize on number line.

examples.trace: Step 1: Plot 3 and 7 on number line. Step 2: Count units between them: 3→4 (1), 4→5 (2), 5→6 (3), 6→7 (4). Step 3: Distance = 4. Alternatively: |3-7| = |-4| = 4.
Step 1: Plot 5 and 2 on number line. Step 2: Count units: 5→4 (1), 4→3 (2), 3→2 (3). Step 3: Distance = 3. Alternatively: |5-2| = |3| = 3.

approaches.name: Max Heap O(n log k)
approaches.summary: Maintain a max heap of size k to store the k closest elements based on difference from target. Iterate through the array, pushing elements and popping the farthest when heap exceeds k. Ideal for streaming data or when k is much smaller than n.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find K Closest Elements Using Max Heap
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find k closest elements to a target using max heap approach.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to maintain a max heap that always contains the k closest elements seen so far. Since we want the k closest elements, we need to keep track of the smallest differences from the target. A max heap allows us to efficiently remove the element with the largest difference (the farthest element) when we have more than k candidates. This works because at any point, the heap contains the k smallest differences encountered. The algorithm iterates through all elements, calculates the absolute difference from the target, and manages the heap. After processing all elements, the heap contains exactly the k closest elements. The time complexity is O(n log k) because we perform heap operations (push/pop) for each of n elements, each taking O(log k) time. Space complexity is O(k) for storing the heap. This approach is particularly efficient when k is much smaller than n, and it can handle streaming data since it processes elements one by one. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the key subroutine of maintaining the max heap with size constraint. Common mistakes include: 1) Not using absolute values for distance calculation, 2) Forgetting to handle the heap size constraint properly, 3) Not considering tie-breaking rules (e.g., smaller element first when distances are equal). Real code pattern: Initialize empty max heap (using negative distances for max heap simulation in languages with min heap only). For each element in array: calculate distance = abs(element - target); push (distance, element) to heap; if heap size > k: pop the element with largest distance. After loop, extract elements from heap and sort them for output.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty max heap. Step 2: Process element 1: distance = |1-3| = 2, heap = [(2,1)], size=1 ≤ k=4, keep. Step 3: Process 2: distance=1, heap = [(2,1),(1,2)], size=2 ≤ 4. Step 4: Process 3: distance=0, heap = [(2,1),(1,2),(0,3)], size=3 ≤ 4. Step 5: Process 4: distance=1, heap = [(2,1),(1,2),(0,3),(1,4)], size=4 ≤ 4. Step 6: Process 5: distance=2, push (2,5): heap = [(2,5),(2,1),(1,2),(0,3),(1,4)], size=5 > 4, pop largest distance: (2,5) removed (since max heap, largest distance 2, and between (2,5) and (2,1), (2,5) might be popped based on implementation). Heap after pop: [(2,1),(1,2),(0,3),(1,4)]. Step 7: Extract elements: [1,2,3,4], sort: [1,2,3,4].

Step 1: Initialize heap. Step 2: Process 1: distance=8, heap=[(8,1)], size=1 ≤ 3. Step 3: Process 1: distance=8, heap=[(8,1),(8,1)], size=2 ≤ 3. Step 4: Process 1: distance=8, heap=[(8,1),(8,1),(8,1)], size=3 ≤ 3. Step 5: Process 10: distance=1, push (1,10): heap=[(8,1),(8,1),(8,1),(1,10)], size=4 > 3, pop largest: (8,1) removed, heap=[(8,1),(8,1),(1,10)]. Step 6: Process 10: distance=1, push (1,10): heap=[(8,1),(8,1),(1,10),(1,10)], size=4 > 3, pop largest: (8,1) removed, heap=[(8,1),(1,10),(1,10)]. Step 7: Process 10: distance=1, push (1,10): heap=[(8,1),(1,10),(1,10),(1,10)], size=4 > 3, pop largest: (8,1) removed, heap=[(1,10),(1,10),(1,10)]. Step 8: Extract: [10,10,10], sort: [10,10,10].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintain Max Heap of Size K with Distance Comparisons
ladders.desc: Implement the core heap management logic that maintains k closest elements based on distance.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this subroutine is to dynamically update a collection to always contain the k smallest distances encountered. Using a max heap allows efficient retrieval and removal of the largest distance when the heap exceeds size k. This ensures that after processing each element, the heap holds the current k closest elements. The algorithm works by: for each new element, compute its distance from the target, add it to the heap, and if the heap size exceeds k, remove the element with the maximum distance. This guarantees that only the closest elements remain. Time complexity per element is O(log k) for heap operations, and overall for n elements it would be O(n log k), but this subroutine focuses on the per-element process. Space is O(k). Connection to L0: This subroutine is the iterative core of the full solution. Common mistakes: 1) Incorrect heap type (should be max heap for distances, but many libraries have min heap only, requiring negative distances), 2) Not handling ties in distance properly (e.g., if distances are equal, might need to prioritize smaller elements), 3) Forgetting to check heap size after each insertion. Real code pattern: Initialize heap as empty list. For each element x: dist = abs(x - target); push (-dist, x) to heap (using negative for max heap); if len(heap) > k: heappop(heap). After loop, heap contains k closest elements as (-dist, x) pairs.

examples.input: Processing elements: [10,20,30] with k=2, target=25, current heap empty
examples.output: Heap after all: contains distances 5 and 5 for elements 20 and 30
examples.trace: Start: heap=[]. Step 1: Process 10: dist=|10-25|=15, push (-15,10): heap=[(-15,10)], size=1 ≤ 2. Step 2: Process 20: dist=5, push (-5,20): heap=[(-15,10),(-5,20)], size=2 ≤ 2. Step 3: Process 30: dist=5, push (-5,30): heap=[(-15,10),(-5,20),(-5,30)], size=3 > 2, pop max: (-15,10) removed (largest negative distance = -15 corresponds to largest positive distance 15). Heap after: [(-5,20),(-5,30)].

examples.input: Processing elements: [5,15,25] with k=1, target=10, current heap empty
examples.trace: Start: heap=[]. Step 1: Process 5: dist=5, push (-5,5): heap=[(-5,5)], size=1 ≤ 1. Step 2: Process 15: dist=5, push (-5,15): heap=[(-5,5),(-5,15)], size=2 > 1, pop max: both have -5, so one is popped (e.g., (-5,5)), heap=[(-5,15)]. Step 3: Process 25: dist=15, push (-15,25): heap=[(-5,15),(-15,25)], size=2 > 1, pop max: (-5,15) removed (since -5 > -15), heap=[(-15,25)].

testCases.input: elements=[1,2,3], k=2, target=2, start heap empty
elements=[10,20,30], k=1, target=15, start heap empty
elements=[5,10,15], k=3, target=12, start heap empty
elements=[100,200,300], k=2, target=250, start heap empty
elements=[1,4,6,8], k=2, target=5, start heap empty
elements=[7,14,21], k=1, target=10, start heap empty
elements=[2,4,6,8], k=3, target=5, start heap empty
elements=[50,60,70], k=2, target=55, start heap empty
elements=[3,6,9], k=2, target=7, start heap empty
elements=[12,24,36], k=1, target=20, start heap empty

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Max Heap to Track K Smallest Distances
ladders.desc: Understand the technique of using a max heap to maintain the k smallest values in a stream.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that a max heap can efficiently manage the k smallest elements from a dataset by always keeping the largest of these k elements at the root. When a new element arrives, if it is smaller than the root (i.e., its distance is less than the current maximum distance in the heap), we replace the root with the new element to maintain the k smallest. This works because the max heap property ensures the root is the largest element in the heap, so by comparing with the root, we can decide if the new element belongs in the set of k smallest. The intuition: imagine you have a list of the k smallest distances so far; the worst (largest) among them is at the top of the max heap. Any new distance that is smaller than this worst should replace it, improving the set. This technique is optimal for streaming data where you cannot store all elements. Time per operation is O(log k) for heap insert/delete. Space is O(k). Connection to L1: This logic is applied in the subroutine by pushing all elements and popping when size exceeds k, which implicitly implements this comparison. Common mistakes: 1) Misunderstanding heap order (max vs min), 2) Not realizing that popping the root after insertion when size > k achieves the comparison, 3) Overcomplicating by explicitly comparing with root before insertion. Real code pattern: For a max heap 'heap' of size at most k: for new distance d: if len(heap) < k: push d; else: if d < heap[0] (root): pop root, push d.

examples.output: After stream: heap contains distances 1 and 2
examples.trace: Start: heap=[] max heap. Step 1: distance 5, heap size 0 < k=2, push 5: heap=[5]. Step 2: distance 1, size 1 < 2, push 1: heap=[5,1] (max heap: root 5). Step 3: distance 3, size 2 == k, compare 3 < root 5? Yes, pop root 5, push 3: heap=[3,1] (re-heapify: root 3). Step 4: distance 2, size 2 == k, compare 2 < root 3? Yes, pop root 3, push 2: heap=[2,1] (root 2). Final: distances 1 and 2.

Start: heap=[]. Step 1: 10, size 0 < 3, push 10: heap=[10]. Step 2: 8, size 1 < 3, push 8: heap=[10,8] (root 10). Step 3: 12, size 2 < 3, push 12: heap=[12,8,10] (root 12). Step 4: 6, size 3 == k, compare 6 < root 12? Yes, pop 12, push 6: heap=[10,8,6] (root 10). Step 5: 9, size 3 == k, compare 9 < root 10? Yes, pop 10, push 9: heap=[9,8,6] (root 9). Final: 6,8,9.

ladders.title: Building Block — Heap Operations: Insert and Extract-Max
ladders.desc: Learn how to perform insert and extract-max operations on a max heap.
ladders.explanation: A max heap is a complete binary tree where each node is greater than or equal to its children, ensuring the maximum element is at the root. The intuition is to maintain this structure efficiently so that we can quickly access the max and update the heap after changes. Insertion: Add the new element at the end of the heap (to maintain completeness), then 'bubble up' by comparing with its parent and swapping if larger, until the heap property is restored. This takes O(log n) time where n is heap size. Extract-max: Remove the root (max element), replace it with the last element in the heap, then 'bubble down' by comparing with children and swapping with the larger child until the heap property is restored, also O(log n). These operations are fundamental for managing a dynamic set where we need frequent access to the maximum. Connection to L2: These operations enable the core logic of maintaining k smallest distances by allowing efficient updates. Common mistakes: 1) Incorrect bubble-up/down logic, 2) Not handling heap as an array properly (indexing starts at 0 or 1), 3) Forgetting to maintain complete tree property. Real code pattern for max heap array 'heap' (0-indexed): Insert(val): heap.append(val); i = len(heap)-1; while i > 0 and heap[i] > heap[(i-1)//2]: swap heap[i] and heap[(i-1)//2]; i = (i-1)//2. Extract_max(): if heap empty return None; max_val = heap[0]; heap[0] = heap[-1]; heap.pop(); i = 0; while True: left = 2*i+1; right = 2*i+2; largest = i; if left < len(heap) and heap[left] > heap[largest]: largest = left; if right < len(heap) and heap[right] > heap[largest]: largest = right; if largest == i: break; swap heap[i] and heap[largest]; i = largest; return max_val.

examples.trace: Start: heap=[10,5,8,3,1] as array representation of tree: root 10, children 5 and 8, etc. Step 1: Append 15: heap=[10,5,8,3,1,15]. Step 2: Bubble up: index i=5, parent index (5-1)//2=2, heap[5]=15 > heap[2]=8? Yes, swap: heap=[10,5,15,3,1,8]. i=2, parent index (2-1)//2=0, heap[2]=15 > heap[0]=10? Yes, swap: heap=[15,5,10,3,1,8]. i=0, stop. Final heap.

Start: heap=[12,8,10,3,5,7]. Step 1: max_val=12. Step 2: Replace root with last element: heap[0]=7, heap=[7,8,10,3,5,7], pop last: heap=[7,8,10,3,5]. Step 3: Bubble down: i=0, left=1 (8), right=2 (10), largest=0. Compare: heap[1]=8 > heap[0]=7? Yes, largest=1. heap[2]=10 > heap[1]=8? Yes, largest=2. Swap heap[0] and heap[2]: heap=[10,8,7,3,5]. i=2, left=5 (index 5 out of bounds), right=6 out, stop. Final heap.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Calculate Absolute Difference and Compare Values
ladders.desc: Compute the absolute difference between two numbers and use it for comparisons.
ladders.explanation: The absolute difference measures how far apart two numbers are on the number line, regardless of direction. For a target value T and an element X, the absolute difference is |X - T|. This is crucial for determining 'closeness' because we care about distance, not whether the element is above or below the target. The intuition: in problems involving proximity, absolute difference provides a non-negative metric that increases as elements move away from the target. Comparing these differences allows us to rank elements by closeness. For example, if target is 10, elements 8 and 12 both have absolute difference 2, so they are equally close. This operation is foundational for the heap approach because we use these differences as keys in the heap. Time complexity is O(1) per calculation. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used when inserting into the heap to compute the distance value. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to take absolute value, leading to negative distances, 2) Misinterpreting distance as squared difference or other metrics, 3) Not handling integer overflow in large numbers (though rare in typical problems). Real code pattern: In Python, abs(x - target). For comparisons, if distances are equal, we might need a tie-breaker (e.g., prefer smaller element).

examples.input: Calculate absolute differences for elements [3,7,10] with target 5
examples.trace: Step 1: For element 3: |3-5| = 2. Step 2: For 7: |7-5| = 2. Step 3: For 10: |10-5| = 5.

examples.input: Compare elements 4 and 6 with target 5, using absolute difference
examples.output: Both have difference 1, so equally close
examples.trace: Step 1: For 4: |4-5| = 1. Step 2: For 6: |6-5| = 1. Step 3: Since 1 == 1, they are equally close to target 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Absolute Value and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Learn what absolute value is and how to perform simple subtraction.
ladders.explanation: Absolute value is a fundamental mathematical concept that represents the distance of a number from zero on the number line, always non-negative. For a real number x, the absolute value |x| is defined as: x if x ≥ 0, or -x if x < 0. Intuitively, it 'removes' the negative sign, giving the magnitude. For example, |5| = 5 and |-5| = 5. Subtraction is the arithmetic operation of finding the difference between two numbers. For numbers a and b, a - b gives how much a is greater than b (if positive) or less than b (if negative). Combining these, |a - b| gives the absolute difference, which is the distance between a and b regardless of order. This is essential for measuring closeness because we want to know how far apart two numbers are, not which is larger. Connection to L4: This foundation enables the calculation of absolute differences. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing absolute value with other operations like square, 2) Misapplying absolute value to expressions (e.g., |a - b| is not |a| - |b|), 3) Forgetting that absolute value always yields a non-negative result. Real code pattern: In programming, use abs() function or implement manually: if x < 0: return -x else: return x.

examples.trace: Step 1: Subtract: 8 - 12 = -4. Step 2: Apply absolute value: |-4| = 4.
Step 1: Subtract: 5 - 5 = 0. Step 2: Apply absolute value: |0| = 0.

approaches.name: Two Pointer with Binary Search O(log n + k)
approaches.summary: First use binary search to find the closest element to the target, then expand outward with two pointers to collect k closest elements. Best when the array is sorted and k is relatively small.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if output not counted, else O(k)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find K Closest Elements Using Two Pointer with Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution: find k closest elements to target in sorted array using binary search to locate starting point, then two pointers to expand outward.
ladders.explanation: This approach efficiently solves the problem by leveraging the sorted nature of the array. First, we use binary search (O(log n)) to find the element closest to the target or the insertion point where target would be placed. This gives us a starting index. Then, we use two pointers (left and right) to expand outward from this starting point, comparing distances to target to select the k closest elements (O(k)). The intuition is that the k closest elements must be contiguous in the sorted array because if you have a non-contiguous set, you could replace an outer element with a closer inner one. The algorithm: 1. Use binary search to find the index where target would be inserted (closest element). 2. Initialize left = index-1, right = index. 3. While we need more elements (k > 0): compare distances of arr[left] and arr[right] to target (handle boundary cases). Pick the closer one and move pointer accordingly. 4. Collect results from arr[left+1:right] (since left and right are exclusive after expansion). Time: O(log n + k) for binary search and pointer expansion. Space: O(1) if not counting output, else O(k) for result list. Connection to L1: This integrates the binary search helper (L1) with the two-pointer expansion logic. Common mistakes: Not handling edge cases where left or right go out of bounds, incorrect distance comparison (use absolute difference), or off-by-one errors in pointer movement. Real code pattern: def findClosestElements(arr, k, x): left = bisect_left(arr, x) - 1; right = left + 1; while k > 0: if left < 0: right += 1; elif right >= len(arr): left -= 1; else: if x - arr[left] <= arr[right] - x: left -= 1; else: right += 1; k -= 1; return arr[left+1:right].

examples.trace: Step 1: Binary search finds index 2 (value 3) as closest to x=3. left=1, right=2. k=4. Step 2: k=4: left=1 (val 2, dist 1), right=2 (val 3, dist 0). right is closer, pick arr[2]=3, move right to 3. Result so far: [3]. k=3. Step 3: k=3: left=1 (val 2, dist 1), right=3 (val 4, dist 1). Tie, pick left (per rule: <=), pick arr[1]=2, move left to 0. Result: [2,3]. k=2. Step 4: k=2: left=0 (val 1, dist 2), right=3 (val 4, dist 1). right is closer, pick arr[3]=4, move right to 4. Result: [2,3,4]. k=1. Step 5: k=1: left=0 (val 1, dist 2), right=4 (val 5, dist 2). Tie, pick left, pick arr[0]=1, move left to -1. Result: [1,2,3,4]. k=0. Final: arr[left+1:right] = arr[0:4] = [1,2,3,4].

Step 1: Binary search finds insertion index 3 (between 5 and 7). left=2, right=3. k=3. Step 2: k=3: left=2 (val 5, dist 1), right=3 (val 7, dist 1). Tie, pick left, pick arr[2]=5, move left to 1. Result: [5]. k=2. Step 3: k=2: left=1 (val 3, dist 3), right=3 (val 7, dist 1). right is closer, pick arr[3]=7, move right to 4. Result: [5,7]. k=1. Step 4: k=1: left=1 (val 3, dist 3), right=4 (val 9, dist 3). Tie, pick left, pick arr[1]=3, move left to 0. Result: [3,5,7]. k=0. Final: arr[left+1:right] = arr[1:4] = [3,5,7].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search to Find Closest Element or Insertion Point
ladders.desc: Use binary search to locate the index of the element closest to target in sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is critical because it efficiently finds the starting point for the two-pointer expansion in O(log n) time. The intuition: in a sorted array, the closest element to a target x will be at the insertion point where x would be placed to maintain sorted order, or one of its neighbors. We use a modified binary search that compares distances to find the exact closest index. Algorithm: 1. Initialize low=0, high=len(arr)-1. 2. While low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2. If arr[mid] == x, return mid (exact match). Else if arr[mid] < x, low = mid+1; else high = mid-1. 3. After loop, low is the insertion index. Then, check neighbors: if low == 0, return 0; if low == len(arr), return len(arr)-1; else compare distances of arr[low-1] and arr[low] to x, return index with smaller distance. Time: O(log n) for binary search. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This provides the 'index' used to initialize left and right pointers in the full problem. Common mistakes: Not handling empty array, off-by-one in insertion index, or incorrect distance comparison when target is outside array bounds. Real code pattern: def find_closest_index(arr, x): if not arr: return -1; low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == x: return mid; elif arr[mid] < x: low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; if low == 0: return 0; if low == len(arr): return len(arr)-1; return low-1 if x - arr[low-1] <= arr[low] - x else low.

examples.trace: Step 1: low=0, high=4. mid=2, arr[2]=3 equals x=3, so return 2.

Step 1: low=0, high=4. mid=2, arr[2]=5 < 6, so low=3. Step 2: low=3, high=4, mid=3, arr[3]=7 > 6, so high=2. Now low=3 > high=2, exit. Compare neighbors arr[2]=5 and arr[3]=7: both are distance 1 from x=6. Tie, prefer left, so return index 2 (value 5).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two Pointer Expansion to Collect K Closest Elements
ladders.desc: Expand outward from a starting index using two pointers to select k elements based on distance to target.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique after finding the closest index. The intuition: the k closest elements must be contiguous around the target in a sorted array. We use left and right pointers starting adjacent to the closest index, and repeatedly pick the closer element by comparing distances, moving the pointer inward. Algorithm: 1. Initialize left = closest_index - 1, right = closest_index. 2. While k > 0: if left < 0, pick arr[right], right++. elif right >= len(arr), pick arr[left], left--. else: if x - arr[left] <= arr[right] - x, pick arr[left], left--; else pick arr[right], right++. Decrement k. 3. The result is arr[left+1:right] (since left and right point to elements just outside the selected range). Time: O(k) as we make k comparisons. Space: O(1) if not counting output. Connection to L1: This uses the index from binary search as starting point. Common mistakes: Incorrectly updating pointers, not handling boundaries, or off-by-one in slice indices. Real code pattern: def expand_from_index(arr, k, x, start_idx): left, right = start_idx-1, start_idx; while k > 0: if left < 0: right += 1; elif right >= len(arr): left -= 1; else: if x - arr[left] <= arr[right] - x: left -= 1; else: right += 1; k -= 1; return arr[left+1:right].

examples.trace: Start: left=1, right=2, k=4. Step 1: k=4, left=1 (val 2, dist 1), right=2 (val 3, dist 0). right closer, pick arr[2]=3, right=3. k=3. Step 2: k=3, left=1 (dist 1), right=3 (val 4, dist 1). Tie, pick left (since 3-2=1 <= 4-3=1), pick arr[1]=2, left=0. k=2. Step 3: k=2, left=0 (val 1, dist 2), right=3 (dist 1). right closer, pick arr[3]=4, right=4. k=1. Step 4: k=1, left=0 (dist 2), right=4 (val 5, dist 2). Tie, pick left, pick arr[0]=1, left=-1. k=0. Result slice: arr[-1+1:4] = arr[0:4] = [1,2,3,4].

Start: left=1, right=2, k=3. Step 1: k=3, left=1 (val 3, dist 3), right=2 (val 5, dist 1). right closer, pick arr[2]=5, right=3. k=2. Step 2: k=2, left=1 (dist 3), right=3 (val 7, dist 1). right closer, pick arr[3]=7, right=4. k=1. Step 3: k=1, left=1 (dist 3), right=4 (val 9, dist 3). Tie, pick left (since 6-3=3 <= 9-6=3), pick arr[1]=3, left=0. k=0. Result slice: arr[0+1:4] = arr[1:4] = [3,5,7].

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare Distances and Handle Ties for Element Selection
ladders.desc: Determine which of two elements is closer to a target, with tie-breaking rules.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for the two-pointer expansion. The intuition: when choosing between two candidate elements, we compute their absolute distances to the target x. The element with smaller distance is closer. If distances are equal, we need a tie-breaking rule; typically, prefer the smaller element (since we want ascending order and smaller elements are left in sorted array). Algorithm: Given two indices i and j in sorted array arr, and target x: 1. Compute dist_i = |x - arr[i]|, dist_j = |x - arr[j]|. 2. If dist_i < dist_j, choose i. Else if dist_j < dist_i, choose j. Else (tie): if arr[i] <= arr[j], choose i (since array is sorted, i < j implies arr[i] <= arr[j]). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This comparison is used in each step of the two-pointer expansion to decide which pointer to move. Common mistakes: Using non-absolute distance for unsorted scenarios (but array is sorted, so x - arr[i] works if i left of target? Actually, we use absolute or signed based on position: in expansion, left is <= target, right is >= target, so x - arr[left] is non-negative and arr[right] - x is non-negative, so we can compare these directly without abs). Real code pattern: def choose_closer(arr, left, right, x): if left < 0: return right; if right >= len(arr): return left; if x - arr[left] <= arr[right] - x: return left; else: return right.

examples.trace: Compute: x - arr[left] = 3-2=1, arr[right] - x = 3-3=0. Since 1 > 0, right is closer (dist 0 vs 1).
Compute: x - arr[left] = 6-3=3, arr[right] - x = 9-6=3. Tie (both 3). Since arr[left] <= arr[right] (3 <= 9), choose left.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Distance Between Number and Target
ladders.desc: Calculate how far a number is from a target value, using absolute difference.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in the algorithm to compare elements. The intuition: distance measures how close a number is to the target; smaller distance means closer. For two numbers a and target x, the distance is |a - x| (absolute difference). In a sorted array, if a <= x, then distance = x - a; if a >= x, distance = a - x. But absolute works always. Algorithm: Given a and x, return abs(a - x). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This distance computation is the core of the comparison in choose_closer. Common mistakes: Forgetting to use absolute value, which can give negative distances and incorrect comparisons. Real code pattern: def distance(a, x): return abs(a - x).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic idea of sorted arrays and how to access elements by index.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a sorted array is a list of numbers arranged in non-decreasing order (each element is less than or equal to the next). Indexing allows us to access elements by position, starting from 0. The intuition: in a sorted array, if you know an index i, you can quickly find neighbors (i-1 and i+1), and elements are ordered so that comparisons are meaningful. For example, if arr = [1,3,5,7], then arr[0]=1, arr[1]=3, etc. This property is exploited in binary search and two-pointer techniques. Algorithm: No complex algorithm here; just understand that arr[i] gives the element at position i, and i must be between 0 and len(arr)-1. Time: O(1) to access an element. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Distance computation uses these indexed elements. Common mistakes: Accessing out-of-bounds indices, which causes errors. Real code pattern: In Python, arr = [1,2,3]; val = arr[0] # gives 1.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0:1, 1:2, 2:3, 3:4, 4:5. So arr[2] is 3.
arr[1] is the second element: 20.
